senses.definition: A thin tube with a camera and light that doctors put into a joint to look inside and sometimes do surgery.
examples: The surgeon used an arthroscope to examine the damage in her knee.
A small arthroscope is inserted through a cut near the joint.
Modern arthroscopes allow doctors to see the joint on a video screen.

senses.definition: Plural of arthroscope: thin tubes with a camera and light that doctors put into joints to look inside and sometimes do surgery.
examples: Smaller arthroscopes are used for joints in the hand and wrist.
New digital arthroscopes provide clearer images of the joint.
Different arthroscopes are designed for different joints.

senses.definition: Relating to or done using an arthroscope, usually through very small cuts around a joint.
examples: He had arthroscopic surgery on his shoulder.
Arthroscopic techniques can reduce pain and recovery time.
The doctor recommended an arthroscopic procedure to repair the torn ligament.

senses.definition: Plural of arthroscopy: operations where doctors look inside joints with an arthroscope and may repair them at the same time.
examples: Thousands of knee arthroscopies are performed every year.
Shoulder arthroscopies are often done as day surgeries.
Modern arthroscopies usually require only a short hospital stay.

senses.definition: A type of surgery where a small camera is put into a joint through a tiny cut so the doctor can look inside and sometimes repair it.
examples: He is having an arthroscopy to treat his torn meniscus.
Arthroscopy is commonly used for knee and shoulder problems.
After the arthroscopy, she went home the same day.

senses.definition: Plural of arthrosis: conditions where joints slowly wear out and become painful and stiff.
examples: The scans showed severe arthroses in both knees.
Elderly patients often suffer from multiple arthroses.
Different arthroses can affect the spine, hips, and hands.

senses.definition: Abnormal or diseased condition of a joint.
examples: The researcher focused on rare forms of arthrosia.
Chronic arthrosia can seriously reduce quality of life.
The textbook described several types of arthrosia in detail.

senses.definition: A long-term joint disease where the cartilage wears down, causing pain and stiffness, especially in older people.
examples: Arthrosis of the knee made it hard for him to walk.
Weight loss can help reduce symptoms of arthrosis.
The X-ray confirmed advanced arthrosis in her hip.

senses.definition: A type of spore formed by breaking up a fungal or bacterial thread into separate cells.
examples: The fungus produces an arthrospore at the end of each hypha.
Under the microscope, arthrospores appeared as short rectangular cells.
The pathogen spreads mainly through resistant arthrospores.

senses.definition: Plural of arthrospore: spores formed when a fungal or bacterial thread breaks into separate cells.
examples: These fungi spread through airborne arthrospores.
Arthrospores were visible as chains of rectangular cells.
The soil sample contained numerous arthrospores.

senses.definition: Producing or relating to arthrospores.
examples: The arthrosporic form of the fungus is responsible for infection.
Arthrosporic bacteria can survive in dry conditions.
This arthrosporic stage allows the organism to spread easily.

senses.definition: Having or forming arthrospores.
examples: Arthrosporous fungi are common in dry, dusty environments.
The species is arthrosporous during part of its life cycle.
Arthrosporous structures were observed along the hyphae.

senses.definition: Inflammation of the bony parts of a joint.
examples: Arthrosteitis may lead to chronic joint pain.
The X-ray findings were consistent with arthrosteitis.
Untreated arthrosteitis can damage the joint surface.

senses.definition: A small supporting structure in certain jointed organisms, mentioned in specialized scientific descriptions.
examples: The arthrosterigma connects adjacent segments.
A tiny arthrosterigma was visible under high magnification.
The paper described the shape of the arthrosterigma in detail.

senses.definition: The mouth opening in certain jointed animals or parasites, described in technical biology.
examples: The arthrostome is armed with tiny teeth.
Damage to the arthrostome prevents the parasite from feeding.
The species is identified partly by the shape of its arthrostome.

senses.definition: A surgical operation in which a joint is cut open or a new opening is made in it.
examples: Arthrostomy was performed to drain the infected joint.
In severe cases, arthrostomy may be necessary.
The surgeon discussed the risks of arthrostomy with the patient.

senses.definition: A former scientific group name for certain crustaceans with jointed shells, used in older classifications.
examples: The term Arthrostraca appears in early zoological literature.
Some textbooks still mention Arthrostraca as a historical group.
Arthrostraca included several orders of small crustaceans.

senses.definition: Inflammation of both a joint and its lining membrane.
examples: Arthrosynovitis can cause pain, swelling, and stiffness.
The patient was diagnosed with chronic arthrosynovitis.
Infectious arthrosynovitis requires prompt antibiotic treatment.

senses.definition: An abnormal tube-like connection involving a joint, described in specialized medical texts.
examples: The surgeon identified an arthrosyrinx leading from the joint.
An arthrosyrinx may form after repeated infections.
Imaging revealed a small arthrosyrinx near the elbow.

senses.definition: A surgical instrument used to cut into a joint.
examples: The surgeon selected an arthrotome for the procedure.
An arthrotome is designed to open the joint capsule safely.
Early arthrotomes were simple knife-like instruments.

senses.definition: Plural of arthrotomy: surgical operations where a joint is opened.
examples: Before arthroscopy, open arthrotomies were more common.
The study compared outcomes after arthrotomies and arthroscopies.
Several arthrotomies had been performed on the damaged knee.

senses.definition: A surgical operation in which a joint is cut open to examine, clean, or repair it.
examples: An arthrotomy was needed to remove the foreign body from the joint.
Open arthrotomy has largely been replaced by arthroscopy.
The surgeon discussed the risks of arthrotomy with the patient.

senses.definition: Injury to a joint caused by a sudden force or repeated stress.
examples: Sports accidents are a common cause of arthrotrauma.
Acute arthrotrauma may damage the cartilage.
The study examined arthrotrauma in young athletes.

senses.definition: Having a special tendency to attack or affect the joints.
examples: Some viruses are arthrotropic and cause joint pain.
The arthrotropic nature of the bacteria leads to arthritis-like symptoms.
Researchers studied arthrotropic infections in animals.

senses.definition: Resembling typhoid fever but mainly affecting the joints, described in older medical literature.
examples: The patient showed arthrotyphoid symptoms after infection.
Arthrotyphoid forms of the disease were recorded in past outbreaks.
The report mentioned an arthrotyphoid pattern of fever and joint pain.

senses.definition: Having joints, or having clear joints or divisions, especially in scientific descriptions.
examples: The arthrous segments of the limb were easily seen.
Arthrous structures allow greater flexibility.
The worm has an arthrous body divided into many rings.

senses.definition: Abnormal dryness in a joint.
examples: Arthroxerosis may lead to friction and pain.
The study described arthroxerosis in elderly patients.
Arthroxerosis can damage the cartilage over time.

senses.definition: A former or broad scientific name for jointed animals such as insects and crustaceans.
examples: The term Arthrozoa was once used for all jointed animals.
Old classifications placed many species within Arthrozoa.
Arthrozoa includes organisms with segmented, jointed bodies.

senses.definition: Relating to Arthrozoa or to jointed animals.
examples: Arthrozoan features are seen in many invertebrates.
The fossil shows typical arthrozoan segmentation.
Arthrozoan groups dominate many ecosystems.

senses.definition: An animal belonging to Arthrozoa, especially a jointed invertebrate.
examples: Each arthrozoan in the sample was carefully measured.
The arthrozoan is adapted to life on the sea floor.
Several arthrozoans were preserved in the rock layer.

senses.definition: Having the character of or relating to jointed animals.
examples: The arthrozoic characteristics suggested an arthropod ancestor.
Arthrozoic traits include segmented limbs and hard exoskeletons.
The fossil shows a clearly arthrozoic body plan.

senses.definition: A male first name.
examples: Arthur is meeting us at the restaurant.
Her grandfather was called Arthur.
Arthur sent me a message this morning.

senses.definition: In stories, especially British ones, King Arthur, a legendary king who led the Knights of the Round Table.
examples: The stories about Arthur and the Round Table are very famous.
Arthur is often shown pulling a sword from a stone.
Many films have been made about King Arthur.

senses.definition: Connected with King Arthur or the stories about him and his knights.
examples: She is writing a novel set in the Arthurian world.
The poem is full of Arthurian images and themes.
Arthurian legends have inspired many modern writers.

senses.definition: Books, studies, and other material related to King Arthur and the stories about him.
examples: The library has an impressive collection of Arthuriana.
She is an expert in modern Arthuriana.
New Arthuriana continues to appear every year.

senses.definition: A Hindu religious ritual in which light from lamps is offered to a god, goddess, or holy person.
examples: The family performed arti at the small temple in their home.
We joined the evening arti by the river.
The priest began the arti, waving the lamp in circles.

senses.definition: Having limbs or parts arranged in pairs, used in specialized scientific descriptions.
examples: The species shows an artiad pattern of limb development.
Artiad structures can be seen in the fossil record.
The shell has an artiad arrangement of spines.

senses.definition: A large truck that has two parts, a cab and a long trailer, joined together so they can bend in the middle.
examples: An artic blocked the road after the accident.
He drives an artic across Europe.
The depot was full of parked artics.

senses.definition: Relating to the Arctic region, used as a short or variant form in some contexts.
examples: They studied artic wildlife in the far north.
Artic conditions made travel difficult.
The team prepared for artic temperatures.

senses.definition: A round green vegetable with many thick leaves, where the soft parts and the centre are eaten after cooking.
examples: We ordered grilled artichoke as a starter.
She showed me how to eat an artichoke leaf by leaf.
Artichoke hearts are used in many salads.

senses.definition: Plural of artichoke: round green vegetables with many thick leaves, where the soft parts and the centre are eaten after cooking.
examples: We bought two large artichokes for dinner.
She added chopped artichokes to the pasta.
Artichokes grow well in this climate.

senses.definition: A piece of writing in a newspaper, magazine, or on the internet about a particular subject.
examples: I read an interesting article about climate change.
She wrote an article for a travel magazine.
The article explains how to save money.

senses.definition: A thing, especially one of a particular type, often used in formal or legal English.
examples: The shop sells household articles.
Stolen articles were found in his car.
Each article must be checked before shipping.

senses.definition: A word like "a", "an", or "the" used before a noun in English grammar.
examples: In this sentence, "the" is a definite article.
Students often forget to use an article before nouns.
She explained the difference between definite and indefinite articles.

senses.definition: A numbered section in a legal document, agreement, or law.
examples: Article 10 of the contract covers payment terms.
They argued that this broke Article 5 of the treaty.
The new rules are set out in Article 3.

senses.definition: Having a formal training contract, especially in law or accountancy.
examples: She is an articled clerk at a law firm.
As an articled trainee, he works under a senior lawyer.
Many articled students later become partners.

senses.definition: Past tense of article: to place someone under a formal training contract, especially in law or accountancy.
examples: He was articled to a firm of solicitors in London.
She articled him to a well-known accountant.
Many young graduates were articled to local firms.

senses.definition: Plural of article: pieces of writing in newspapers, magazines, or on the internet about particular subjects.
examples: I like reading articles about science.
The website publishes new articles every day.
She has written several articles on education.

senses.definition: Formal training contracts, especially for lawyers or accountants, used mainly in British English.
examples: She is doing her articles with a large law firm.
He completed his articles before qualifying as a solicitor.
Competition for articles at top firms is very strong.

senses.definition: Third person singular of article: places someone under a formal training contract, especially in law or accountancy.
examples: The firm articles several new graduates each year.
The company articles trainees for a period of three years.
She articles students who want to become solicitors.

senses.definition: A period of formal supervised training in a law firm or similar organization, under a special contract.
examples: Articling is an important stage in becoming a lawyer.
She completed her articling at a small local firm.
The firm offers several articling positions each year.

senses.definition: Present participle of article: doing a period of formal supervised training in a law or accountancy firm.
examples: She is articling with a corporate law firm.
He spent two years articling before he qualified.
Many graduates are articling in big-city firms.

senses.definition: Plural of artic: large trucks that have a cab and a long trailer joined together so they can bend in the middle.
examples: Artics carry most of the goods on this motorway.
The yard was crowded with parked artics.
New rules limit the size of artics in the city centre.

senses.definition: The quality of being easy or possible to pronounce clearly.
examples: The articulability of the new word was tested with learners.
Good articulability helps children learn sounds more quickly.
The language has high articulability for most speakers.

senses.definition: The quality of being able to be expressed in words.
examples: Some feelings resist articulability.
The novel explores the limits of articulability in human experience.
Articulability is central to the philosopher's theory of meaning.

senses.definition: Able to be pronounced clearly.
examples: The invented term is easily articulable.
Children first learn the most articulable sounds.
Some consonant clusters are hardly articulable for learners.

senses.definition: Able to be clearly expressed in words.
examples: Her anger was not yet articulable.
The theory is complex but articulable.
They struggled to make their demands articulable in public debate.

senses.definition: Plural of articulacy: different kinds or examples of the ability to speak or write clearly and effectively.
examples: The book celebrates the many articulacies of working-class writers.
Different articulacies emerge in different social settings.
The teacher encouraged students to develop their own articulacies.

senses.definition: The ability to express your ideas clearly and effectively in speech or writing.
examples: Her articulacy impressed the interview panel.
Good articulacy is important for leadership.
The course aims to improve students' articulacy in English.

senses.definition: Someone or something that expresses an idea, feeling, or group clearly.
examples: She became an important articulant of feminist concerns.
The poet is an articulant of urban experience.
The movement lacked a strong articulant for its message.

senses.definition: Relating to a joint where two bones meet in the body.
examples: Articular surfaces of the bone were worn down.
The disease causes articular pain and stiffness.
Articular cartilage protects the ends of the bones.

senses.definition: A specific bone in the lower jaw of many vertebrate animals, named in anatomical descriptions.
examples: The articulare forms part of the jaw joint in reptiles.
Fossils showed a well-preserved articulare.
The articulare connects with other jaw bones.
